caption:
Quenderin is on the App Store.

A real AI model, running fully on your iPhone — no account, no cloud, nothing leaves the device. Pick a model that fits your phone, download it once, and it works offline: on a plane, in a tunnel, anywhere.

Free. Open source. Every line on GitHub.

The crash that only happened after you'd used the app.

⌘Q → SIGABRT — but only if you'd sent one message first. Eleven byte-identical crash reports in a day. The culprit: llama.cpp's Metal backend asserting inside C++ static destructors at process exit. The fix is one line, and the underscore in _exit(0) is load-bearing.

A model that fits on your phone is smaller than one in a datacenter — and it will sometimes be confidently wrong.

We don't hide that. Every model carries a plain quality grade, the empty state warns you before you start, and we publish the real numbers instead of adjectives. That's the trade: a slice of raw capability, for the network never being in the loop.

This reply was generated with the network off.

No request left the phone — the model is on the device, not a thin client for someone else's datacenter. 15 tokens/sec on an iPhone 12, measured. The whole point: nothing you type ever travels.

Never trust a model file until it's verified.

A model is multi-gigabyte untrusted bytes going into a native C++ parser — textbook attack surface. So there's a hard gate: HTTP status before the first byte, GGUF magic, SHA-256. An App Store reviewer found the one hole we'd missed. Here's the whole story.

We publish exactly how much smaller a local model is.

Not adjectives — measured numbers: tokens/sec on real devices, download sizes, memory. If we're going to ask you to trust the private part, you should be able to check the honest part.

The app checks your hardware before it offers you anything.

RAM, chip, memory budget — then every model is labelled Fits, Tight, or Too big, with the real numbers. It won't hand you a model that would run out of memory. An honest default you can always override.

How the app guesses what your device can run — and admits it's a guess.

It reads your RAM, chip, and (on a phone) the memory budget iOS will grant before it kills the app, then picks the largest model that clears a fitness gate. Total RAM isn't free RAM, so it's honest about the heuristic.

No account. No email. No telemetry. Just the code.

There's no sign-up because there's no server to sign up to. After the one-time model download, an offline-readiness check confirms zero network calls. Don't take our word for it — it's MIT, read it.

On a Mac, Quenderin can do the task — not just answer it.

The computer-use agent works with your files, apps, and Apple Shortcuts, and it shows its work: every tool call in the run log. By design it can never touch payments, deletion, or credentials.

A computer-use agent that never leaves your Mac.

It can operate your machine — but it's local, it logs every action to an on-disk ledger, and undo works even in a new session. Safety as an architectural property, not a promise about model behaviour.

Airplane mode is not a limitation here. It's the point.

On a plane, in a tunnel, in a tent, on a dead-zone commute — the model is already on your device. Nothing to reach, nothing to wait for.

Every model, explained in plain language.

Parameters, download size, memory, quantization — with a glossary behind one button, no jargon assumed. You should know exactly what you're putting on your device before you download 4 GB of it.

iOS and Android don't share a codebase — so how do they stay identical?

The core logic (memory-fitness math, model catalog, download-integrity gate) is hand-ported between Swift and Kotlin. A parity harness makes both platforms produce byte-identical answers for a shared set of test vectors, checked on every commit. It's caught real drift more than once.

What model should we add next?

The catalog is curated for models that actually run well on-device — but we're always looking. Drop the model you want to run locally in the comments, and why.

Not a new phone? Still runs.

The ultra-light build is a 0.4 GB download that runs on modest hardware. Bigger phone, bigger model — your choice, from 0.4 GB up to a ~13 GB mixture-of-experts flagship. The picker tells you honestly what fits.

Apple rejected a build for "an error on model download." They were right.

One catalog entry pointed at a repo that had been renamed — the URL 404'd, and the downloader wrote the error page to disk before checking the status code. Three fixes, plus a CI job that pings every model URL so it can't recur. The honesty: we ship the post-mortem.

Free, and every line is public.

Quenderin is open source under the MIT licence — the probe, the integrity gate, the inference engine, all of it. You can read exactly what runs on your device, and run it yourself from source.

Llama, Qwen, Gemma — your pick, running locally.

A curated catalog of open models, each with a plain quality grade and a real download size. Same one-tap install, same offline result. You choose the trade between size and capability.

The agent grows hands — locally.

Tool use, running on-device: the model can call a calculator, a converter, and more, and it shows each call. The interesting part is the guardrail — whole categories of action are impossible by construction, not by asking the model nicely.

After the download, the model is a file on your disk.

Not a subscription, not a session that expires, not something that can be deprecated out from under you. You downloaded it; it's yours; it runs with no permission from anyone.

An agent that shows its work.

Every tool call is in the run log — the unit converter, the calculator, the steps it took to the answer. No black box. And a hard architectural line it cannot cross: payments, deletion, credentials are off-limits.

Why the Mac app exits with _exit(0) instead of a graceful shutdown.

Because there's nothing to lose at exit: every conversation turn and agent action is persisted continuously, not on quit. So we can skip the C++ teardown that was aborting — it loses nothing and removes the one broken thing in it. Designing around a library that aborts instead of returning.

The honest cost of running a model on your phone.

Sustained generation warms the chip and uses battery, and the context window is smaller than a server's. We'd rather you know the trade-offs up front than discover them mid-task. Private-by-necessity is worth it — but only you can decide that.

Quenderin on Android — the closed beta is open.

Same local-inference core as the iPhone app, rebuilt native for Android. Two steps to get in: join the testers group, then opt in on Google Play. It's an early build — your feedback shapes what ships.

How do you verify a 13 GB model without 13 GB of RAM?

You stream it through the hasher a megabyte at a time, so checking a huge model costs kilobytes, not gigabytes. And the file is written to a .partial path, only renamed into place after it verifies — so no consumer can ever pick a half-written model.

Help push the Android app to the finish line.

Google graduates a closed beta once 12 testers have stayed engaged for 14 days. If you've got an Android phone and a few minutes across two weeks, join the testers group and opt in — that's the whole favour.

Prefer a terminal? Quenderin has one.

The same local model, driven from the command line on your desktop — scriptable, offline, open source. The terminal gets a seat at the table.

The number that decides what model your iPhone can run isn't "total RAM."

It's the jetsam budget — how much memory iOS lets one app hold before it kills it — plus an overhead factor for the KV cache inference needs at runtime. The probe is deliberately pessimistic on phones: better to under-pick than to crash.

Why run an AI locally at all?

Because "it works offline" and "nothing leaves the device" turn out to be the same feature. Private by necessity, available by necessity. For anyone offline, privacy-bound, or just done being metered — that's the entire reason to be here.

Run it on your laptop today.

The desktop app is open source and runs from GitHub on macOS and Linux — the same probe-download-offline flow, the same models. Four lines to clone and go.

A download that can't finish shouldn't start.

Before pulling a multi-GB model, Quenderin does a disk-space preflight and refuses if it won't fit — because running out of space at 90% is a worse experience than a clear "no" up front. Boring engineering. It's most of the job.

"Trust us" is not a security model. The source is.

Everything that decides what runs on your device — and proves nothing leaves it — is open. Star the repo if you want to follow along, or clone it and check for yourself.

Lose Wi-Fi at 80%? It picks up where it left off.

The model download is resumable and Wi-Fi-only by default, so a multi-GB pull never surprises your cellular bill. Small details, because first-run is the one part that isn't instant.

Dense-model RAM math is wrong for mixture-of-experts — so we fixed the math.

A paged MoE flagship can be far larger on disk than the RAM it actually needs to run, because only a few experts are active per token. Getting the fitness heuristic right for MoE is what lets a big model run on hardware that "shouldn't" fit it.

"Nothing leaves your device" is a claim you can check.

Turn on airplane mode and the app still works — because there was never a network call to lose. Zero calls after the one-time model download, and an offline-readiness check that confirms it before you leave signal.

Hit a hard question? It can reach for a bigger model.

If you've installed more than one, the router can suggest a larger model for a task the small one will struggle with — you stay in control, and it stays on-device. Right-sized by default, upgradable on demand.

Cross-platform parity proved the twins agreed. It didn't prove the URL was alive.

All four platforms agreeing on the same dead model link still ships a dead link. So now a CI job does a one-byte ranged GET against every catalog URL on a schedule — rot gets caught by us, before your multi-GB download 404s.

Where Quenderin is going, stage by stage.

iPhone shipped, Android in beta, desktop from source — and the roadmap is public. Local autonomous computer use is the real mission; the private offline chat is the foundation. Read the plan, tell us what's missing.

Want a model that isn't in the catalog? Search for it.

On iPhone you can search open models on Hugging Face, and Quenderin installs them through the same integrity-checked path as the curated ones — same magic-header and checksum gate, same offline result.

Why we ship Q4, not the "full" model.

Quantization shrinks a model by storing its weights in fewer bits. Q4_K_M is the sweet spot on a phone: about a quarter the size, most of the quality. We pick the quant per model and tell you the real download and RAM up front — no "HD" asterisks.

Straight talk: an on-device model has a smaller context window than a server's.

It can't hold as much of a long document or conversation at once. That's a real limit of running locally, and we'd rather name it than let you hit it by surprise. In exchange, the whole thing runs in your hand, offline.

Every model in the app wears its honesty on its sleeve.

A plain-language quality grade sits right next to each one, so you're never guessing whether the tiny fast model is up to your task. Pick with your eyes open — that's the whole idea.

The number iOS never shows you — and the one that decides everything.

A phone has, say, 6 GB of RAM, but the OS will kill your app long before you use it all (jetsam). Total RAM is a lie; the budget is what matters. The app probes for it and sizes the model to survive, not to look impressive.

A quiet thank-you.

Quenderin went from "runs on my machine" to on the App Store and into Android beta — built in the open, by people who read the stack traces. If it's useful to you, a star on GitHub or a share is how it finds the next person who needs an AI that never phones home.

Model spotlight: Qwen3.6 35B MoE.

35B parameters, UD-IQ3_XXS — 13.2 GB download, about 5.5 GB of RAM to run. A mixture-of-experts model: big-model skills on modest RAM. Speaks Russian, English, Chinese + 100 more.

Every token runs on your device. Before you download, the app's picker tells you honestly whether it Fits, is Tight, or is Too big for your hardware.

Why Quenderin runs on the CPU/GPU, not the "AI chip."

The Neural Engine is fast but closed — you can't run an arbitrary GGUF on it today. llama.cpp uses Metal (GPU) and the CPU, which is what makes "bring any model" possible. We'd rather be honest about the trade-off than market a chip we don't use.

We don't have a privacy policy that says "we won't sell your data."

We have one that says there is no data. No account, no telemetry, no server that ever sees your conversations — because there is no server. You can't leak what you never collect.

Model spotlight: Qwen3 14B.

14B parameters, Q4_K_M — 9.0 GB download, about 11 GB of RAM to run. The highest-quality model in the catalog. Speaks Russian, English, Chinese + 100 more.

Every token runs on your device. Before you download, the app's picker tells you honestly whether it Fits, is Tight, or is Too big for your hardware.

Making tokens appear the instant they're generated — without the UI stuttering.

Generation happens on a background thread; the transcript follows along and stops the moment you scroll up to read. Small thing, but it's the difference between "feels native" and "feels like a webview."

The airplane test.

Put your phone in airplane mode and ask it something. It still answers — because the model is on the phone, not in a datacenter you're renting by the token. Works on a flight, in a tunnel, off the grid, or the day the Wi-Fi dies.

Model spotlight: Gemma 4 12B.

12B parameters, Q4_K_M — 7.1 GB download, about 9 GB of RAM to run. Broad multilingual coverage. Speaks Russian, English + 140 more.

Every token runs on your device. Before you download, the app's picker tells you honestly whether it Fits, is Tight, or is Too big for your hardware.

Bring any model. Drag a GGUF in.

The curated catalog is a starting point, not a walled garden — drop in any GGUF from Hugging Face and it runs. Same integrity checks (magic bytes, size) as the catalog models. Your device, your model, your choice.

When you should NOT use a small on-device model.

Medical, legal, or financial decisions. Anything where being confidently wrong is expensive. A 3B model that fits your phone is smaller than a datacenter model and will sometimes miss — we say so, in the app. Honesty is the feature.

Model spotlight: Qwen2.5 Coder 7B.

7B parameters, Q4_K_M — 4.7 GB download, about 6.5 GB of RAM to run. Tuned for writing and explaining code. Speaks Russian, English, Chinese + 25 more.

Every token runs on your device. Before you download, the app's picker tells you honestly whether it Fits, is Tight, or is Too big for your hardware.

"No data leaves the device" — how you can verify it, not just trust it.

Turn on airplane mode and it still works: that's the demo. But the real proof is the code — it's all on GitHub, and there's no analytics SDK, no API client, nothing to phone home. Read it yourself.

What's next isn't a secret.

The roadmap is public — what we're building, what we're weighing, what we've decided against and why. Tell us what matters to you; a roadmap you can argue with is better than a hype thread you can't.

Model spotlight: DeepSeek-R1 7B.

7B parameters, Q4_K_M — 4.7 GB download, about 6.5 GB of RAM to run. A reasoning model — it thinks step by step before it answers. Speaks English & Chinese — weak Russian.

Every token runs on your device. Before you download, the app's picker tells you honestly whether it Fits, is Tight, or is Too big for your hardware.

How the app suggests the right model for a new chat.

Ask for code, it points at your installed coder model; ask a reasoning question, it points at the reasoning one. A one-tap suggestion — never a silent switch behind your back. You're always the one who decides.

Which model should we add next?

New open models ship every week. If there's one you want in the catalog — sized and quantized so it just fits and just runs — tell us. This catalog grows from what people actually ask for.

Model spotlight: Llama 3 8B.

8B parameters, Q4_K_M — 4.7 GB download, about 6.75 GB of RAM to run. The highest-quality model in the catalog. Speaks English-focused — weak Russian.

Every token runs on your device. Before you download, the app's picker tells you honestly whether it Fits, is Tight, or is Too big for your hardware.

One inference core, three platforms.

iPhone, Mac, and desktop all run the same llama.cpp core through a shared layer, so a fix on one lands everywhere and the behaviour matches. Less magic, fewer "works on my device" surprises.

Free, no ads, no account — and here's why that's sustainable.

There's no server to pay for: the model runs on your device, so our costs don't scale with your usage. That's not a growth hack, it's the architecture. It's also why we can be MIT and mean it.

Model spotlight: Mistral 7B.

7B parameters, Q4_K_M — 4.1 GB download, about 6 GB of RAM to run. A fast, capable all-rounder. Speaks English + European — weak Russian.

Every token runs on your device. Before you download, the app's picker tells you honestly whether it Fits, is Tight, or is Too big for your hardware.

The agent asks before it changes anything — by design, not by prompt.

Every action that modifies your machine stops for a yes. It keeps a reviewable per-task log and can undo a whole task, even in a new session. A cloud agent can promise this; a local one can prove it.

You own the model file.

Once it's downloaded it's yours — a plain GGUF on your disk. No subscription that bricks it, no remote kill switch, no "your access has been revoked." Delete the app tomorrow and the model still runs in anything that reads GGUF.

Model spotlight: Gemma 3 4B.

4B parameters, Q4_K_M — 2.5 GB download, about 3.8 GB of RAM to run. Broad multilingual coverage. Speaks Russian, English + 140 more.

Every token runs on your device. Before you download, the app's picker tells you honestly whether it Fits, is Tight, or is Too big for your hardware.

Every release, written down in plain language.

What changed, what broke, what we fixed — the changelog is public and honest, including the embarrassing bits. If you're going to trust software with your private conversations, you should be able to see how it's built over time.

The difference from cloud AI, in one line: nothing you type leaves the device.

Not "encrypted in transit," not "we don't train on your data" — it never travels at all. For a diary, a health question, a work secret, that's not a nice-to-have. That's the whole point.

Model spotlight: Qwen3 4B.

4B parameters, Q4_K_M — 2.4 GB download, about 3.6 GB of RAM to run. A solid all-round on-device chat model. Speaks Russian, English, Chinese + 100 more.

Every token runs on your device. Before you download, the app's picker tells you honestly whether it Fits, is Tight, or is Too big for your hardware.

⌘Q → SIGABRT — but only if you'd sent one message first. Eleven byte-identical crash reports in a day. The culprit: llama.cpp's Metal backend asserting inside C++ static destructors at process exit. The fix is one line, and the underscore in _exit(0) is load-bearing.

We don't hide that. Every model carries a plain quality grade, the empty state warns you before you start, and we publish the real numbers instead of adjectives. That's the trade: a slice of raw capability, for the network never being in the loop.

Model spotlight: Phi-4 Mini 3.8B.

3.8B parameters, Q4_K_M — 2.3 GB download, about 3.4 GB of RAM to run. Tiny and quick — runs on modest phones. Speaks English-focused — weak Russian.

Every token runs on your device. Before you download, the app's picker tells you honestly whether it Fits, is Tight, or is Too big for your hardware.

Not adjectives — measured numbers: tokens/sec on real devices, download sizes, memory. If we're going to ask you to trust the private part, you should be able to check the honest part.

Model spotlight: Llama 3.2 3B.

3B parameters, Q4_K_M — 2.0 GB download, about 3 GB of RAM to run. A solid all-round on-device chat model. Speaks English + 7 more — no Russian.

Every token runs on your device. Before you download, the app's picker tells you honestly whether it Fits, is Tight, or is Too big for your hardware.

There's no sign-up because there's no server to sign up to. After the one-time model download, an offline-readiness check confirms zero network calls. Don't take our word for it — it's MIT, read it.

Model spotlight: Llama 3.2 1B.

1B parameters, Q4_K_M — 0.8 GB download, about 1.5 GB of RAM to run. Tiny and quick — runs on modest phones. Speaks English + 7 more — no Russian.

Every token runs on your device. Before you download, the app's picker tells you honestly whether it Fits, is Tight, or is Too big for your hardware.

On a plane, in a tunnel, in a tent, on a dead-zone commute — the model is already on your device. Nothing to reach, nothing to wait for.

Model spotlight: Llama 3.2 1B Ultra-Light.

1B parameters, Q2_K — 0.4 GB download, about 0.7 GB of RAM to run. Tiny and quick — runs on modest phones. Speaks English + 7 more — no Russian.

Every token runs on your device. Before you download, the app's picker tells you honestly whether it Fits, is Tight, or is Too big for your hardware.

The Neural Engine is fast but closed — you can't run an arbitrary GGUF on it today. llama.cpp uses Metal (GPU) and the CPU, which is what makes "bring any model" possible. We'd rather be honest about the trade-off than market a chip we don't use.

We don't have a privacy policy that says "we won't sell your data."

We have one that says there is no data. No account, no telemetry, no server that ever sees your conversations — because there is no server. You can't leak what you never collect.

Lose Wi-Fi at 80%? It picks up where it left off.

The model download is resumable and Wi-Fi-only by default, so a multi-GB pull never surprises your cellular bill. Small details, because first-run is the one part that isn't instant.

Making tokens appear the instant they're generated — without the UI stuttering.

Generation happens on a background thread; the transcript follows along and stops the moment you scroll up to read. Small thing, but it's the difference between "feels native" and "feels like a webview."

The airplane test.

Put your phone in airplane mode and ask it something. It still answers — because the model is on the phone, not in a datacenter you're renting by the token. Works on a flight, in a tunnel, off the grid, or the day the Wi-Fi dies.

Hit a hard question? It can reach for a bigger model.

If you've installed more than one, the router can suggest a larger model for a task the small one will struggle with — you stay in control, and it stays on-device. Right-sized by default, upgradable on demand.

Bring any model. Drag a GGUF in.

The curated catalog is a starting point, not a walled garden — drop in any GGUF from Hugging Face and it runs. Same integrity checks (magic bytes, size) as the catalog models. Your device, your model, your choice.

When you should NOT use a small on-device model.

Medical, legal, or financial decisions. Anything where being confidently wrong is expensive. A 3B model that fits your phone is smaller than a datacenter model and will sometimes miss — we say so, in the app. Honesty is the feature.

Want a model that isn't in the catalog? Search for it.

On iPhone you can search open models on Hugging Face, and Quenderin installs them through the same integrity-checked path as the curated ones — same magic-header and checksum gate, same offline result.

"No data leaves the device" — how you can verify it, not just trust it.

Turn on airplane mode and it still works: that's the demo. But the real proof is the code — it's all on GitHub, and there's no analytics SDK, no API client, nothing to phone home. Read it yourself.

What's next isn't a secret.

The roadmap is public — what we're building, what we're weighing, what we've decided against and why. Tell us what matters to you; a roadmap you can argue with is better than a hype thread you can't.

Every model in the app wears its honesty on its sleeve.

A plain-language quality grade sits right next to each one, so you're never guessing whether the tiny fast model is up to your task. Pick with your eyes open — that's the whole idea.

How the app suggests the right model for a new chat.

Ask for code, it points at your installed coder model; ask a reasoning question, it points at the reasoning one. A one-tap suggestion — never a silent switch behind your back. You're always the one who decides.

Which model should we add next?

New open models ship every week. If there's one you want in the catalog — sized and quantized so it just fits and just runs — tell us. This catalog grows from what people actually ask for.

Model spotlight: Qwen3.6 35B MoE.

35B parameters, UD-IQ3_XXS — 13.2 GB download, about 5.5 GB of RAM to run. A mixture-of-experts model: big-model skills on modest RAM. Speaks Russian, English, Chinese + 100 more.

Every token runs on your device. Before you download, the app's picker tells you honestly whether it Fits, is Tight, or is Too big for your hardware.

One inference core, three platforms.

iPhone, Mac, and desktop all run the same llama.cpp core through a shared layer, so a fix on one lands everywhere and the behaviour matches. Less magic, fewer "works on my device" surprises.

Free, no ads, no account — and here's why that's sustainable.

There's no server to pay for: the model runs on your device, so our costs don't scale with your usage. That's not a growth hack, it's the architecture. It's also why we can be MIT and mean it.

Model spotlight: Qwen3 14B.

14B parameters, Q4_K_M — 9.0 GB download, about 11 GB of RAM to run. The highest-quality model in the catalog. Speaks Russian, English, Chinese + 100 more.

Every token runs on your device. Before you download, the app's picker tells you honestly whether it Fits, is Tight, or is Too big for your hardware.

The agent asks before it changes anything — by design, not by prompt.

Every action that modifies your machine stops for a yes. It keeps a reviewable per-task log and can undo a whole task, even in a new session. A cloud agent can promise this; a local one can prove it.

You own the model file.

Once it's downloaded it's yours — a plain GGUF on your disk. No subscription that bricks it, no remote kill switch, no "your access has been revoked." Delete the app tomorrow and the model still runs in anything that reads GGUF.

Model spotlight: Gemma 4 12B.

12B parameters, Q4_K_M — 7.1 GB download, about 9 GB of RAM to run. Broad multilingual coverage. Speaks Russian, English + 140 more.

Every token runs on your device. Before you download, the app's picker tells you honestly whether it Fits, is Tight, or is Too big for your hardware.

Every release, written down in plain language.

What changed, what broke, what we fixed — the changelog is public and honest, including the embarrassing bits. If you're going to trust software with your private conversations, you should be able to see how it's built over time.

The difference from cloud AI, in one line: nothing you type leaves the device.

Not "encrypted in transit," not "we don't train on your data" — it never travels at all. For a diary, a health question, a work secret, that's not a nice-to-have. That's the whole point.

Model spotlight: Qwen2.5 Coder 7B.

7B parameters, Q4_K_M — 4.7 GB download, about 6.5 GB of RAM to run. Tuned for writing and explaining code. Speaks Russian, English, Chinese + 25 more.

Every token runs on your device. Before you download, the app's picker tells you honestly whether it Fits, is Tight, or is Too big for your hardware.

The crash that only happened after you'd used the app.

⌘Q → SIGABRT — but only if you'd sent one message first. Eleven byte-identical crash reports in a day. The culprit: llama.cpp's Metal backend asserting inside C++ static destructors at process exit. The fix is one line, and the underscore in _exit(0) is load-bearing.

A model that fits on your phone is smaller than one in a datacenter — and it will sometimes be confidently wrong.

We don't hide that. Every model carries a plain quality grade, the empty state warns you before you start, and we publish the real numbers instead of adjectives. That's the trade: a slice of raw capability, for the network never being in the loop.

Model spotlight: DeepSeek-R1 7B.

7B parameters, Q4_K_M — 4.7 GB download, about 6.5 GB of RAM to run. A reasoning model — it thinks step by step before it answers. Speaks English & Chinese — weak Russian.

Every token runs on your device. Before you download, the app's picker tells you honestly whether it Fits, is Tight, or is Too big for your hardware.

Never trust a model file until it's verified.

A model is multi-gigabyte untrusted bytes going into a native C++ parser — textbook attack surface. So there's a hard gate: HTTP status before the first byte, GGUF magic, SHA-256. An App Store reviewer found the one hole we'd missed. Here's the whole story.

We publish exactly how much smaller a local model is.

Not adjectives — measured numbers: tokens/sec on real devices, download sizes, memory. If we're going to ask you to trust the private part, you should be able to check the honest part.

Model spotlight: Llama 3 8B.

Every token runs on your device. Before you download, the app's picker tells you honestly whether it Fits, is Tight, or is Too big for your hardware.

How the app guesses what your device can run — and admits it's a guess.

It reads your RAM, chip, and (on a phone) the memory budget iOS will grant before it kills the app, then picks the largest model that clears a fitness gate. Total RAM isn't free RAM, so it's honest about the heuristic.

No account. No email. No telemetry. Just the code.

There's no sign-up because there's no server to sign up to. After the one-time model download, an offline-readiness check confirms zero network calls. Don't take our word for it — it's MIT, read it.

Every token runs on your device. Before you download, the app's picker tells you honestly whether it Fits, is Tight, or is Too big for your hardware.

A computer-use agent that never leaves your Mac.

It can operate your machine — but it's local, it logs every action to an on-disk ledger, and undo works even in a new session. Safety as an architectural property, not a promise about model behaviour.

Airplane mode is not a limitation here. It's the point.

On a plane, in a tunnel, in a tent, on a dead-zone commute — the model is already on your device. Nothing to reach, nothing to wait for.

Every token runs on your device. Before you download, the app's picker tells you honestly whether it Fits, is Tight, or is Too big for your hardware.

iOS and Android don't share a codebase — so how do they stay identical?

The core logic (memory-fitness math, model catalog, download-integrity gate) is hand-ported between Swift and Kotlin. A parity harness makes both platforms produce byte-identical answers for a shared set of test vectors, checked on every commit. It's caught real drift more than once.

What model should we add next?

The catalog is curated for models that actually run well on-device — but we're always looking. Drop the model you want to run locally in the comments, and why.

4B parameters, Q4_K_M — 2.4 GB download, about 3.6 GB of RAM to run. A solid all-round on-device chat model. Speaks Russian, English, Chinese + 100 more.

Every token runs on your device. Before you download, the app's picker tells you honestly whether it Fits, is Tight, or is Too big for your hardware.

Apple rejected a build for "an error on model download." They were right.

One catalog entry pointed at a repo that had been renamed — the URL 404'd, and the downloader wrote the error page to disk before checking the status code. Three fixes, plus a CI job that pings every model URL so it can't recur. The honesty: we ship the post-mortem.

Free, and every line is public.

Quenderin is open source under the MIT licence — the probe, the integrity gate, the inference engine, all of it. You can read exactly what runs on your device, and run it yourself from source.

Every token runs on your device. Before you download, the app's picker tells you honestly whether it Fits, is Tight, or is Too big for your hardware.

The agent grows hands — locally.

Tool use, running on-device: the model can call a calculator, a converter, and more, and it shows each call. The interesting part is the guardrail — whole categories of action are impossible by construction, not by asking the model nicely.

After the download, the model is a file on your disk.

Not a subscription, not a session that expires, not something that can be deprecated out from under you. You downloaded it; it's yours; it runs with no permission from anyone.

3B parameters, Q4_K_M — 2.0 GB download, about 3 GB of RAM to run. A solid all-round on-device chat model. Speaks English + 7 more — no Russian.

Every token runs on your device. Before you download, the app's picker tells you honestly whether it Fits, is Tight, or is Too big for your hardware.

Why the Mac app exits with _exit(0) instead of a graceful shutdown.

Because there's nothing to lose at exit: every conversation turn and agent action is persisted continuously, not on quit. So we can skip the C++ teardown that was aborting — it loses nothing and removes the one broken thing in it. Designing around a library that aborts instead of returning.

The honest cost of running a model on your phone.

Sustained generation warms the chip and uses battery, and the context window is smaller than a server's. We'd rather you know the trade-offs up front than discover them mid-task. Private-by-necessity is worth it — but only you can decide that.

Model spotlight: Llama 3.2 1B.

1B parameters, Q4_K_M — 0.8 GB download, about 1.5 GB of RAM to run. Tiny and quick — runs on modest phones. Speaks English + 7 more — no Russian.

Every token runs on your device. Before you download, the app's picker tells you honestly whether it Fits, is Tight, or is Too big for your hardware.

How do you verify a 13 GB model without 13 GB of RAM?

You stream it through the hasher a megabyte at a time, so checking a huge model costs kilobytes, not gigabytes. And the file is written to a .partial path, only renamed into place after it verifies — so no consumer can ever pick a half-written model.

Help push the Android app to the finish line.

Google graduates a closed beta once 12 testers have stayed engaged for 14 days. If you've got an Android phone and a few minutes across two weeks, join the testers group and opt in — that's the whole favour.

Model spotlight: Llama 3.2 1B Ultra-Light.

1B parameters, Q2_K — 0.4 GB download, about 0.7 GB of RAM to run. Tiny and quick — runs on modest phones. Speaks English + 7 more — no Russian.

Every token runs on your device. Before you download, the app's picker tells you honestly whether it Fits, is Tight, or is Too big for your hardware.

The number that decides what model your iPhone can run isn't "total RAM."

It's the jetsam budget — how much memory iOS lets one app hold before it kills it — plus an overhead factor for the KV cache inference needs at runtime. The probe is deliberately pessimistic on phones: better to under-pick than to crash.

Why run an AI locally at all?

Because "it works offline" and "nothing leaves the device" turn out to be the same feature. Private by necessity, available by necessity. For anyone offline, privacy-bound, or just done being metered — that's the entire reason to be here.

Quenderin is on the App Store.

A real AI model, running fully on your iPhone — no account, no cloud, nothing leaves the device. Pick a model that fits your phone, download it once, and it works offline: on a plane, in a tunnel, anywhere.

Free. Open source. Every line on GitHub.

A download that can't finish shouldn't start.

Before pulling a multi-GB model, Quenderin does a disk-space preflight and refuses if it won't fit — because running out of space at 90% is a worse experience than a clear "no" up front. Boring engineering. It's most of the job.

"Trust us" is not a security model. The source is.

Everything that decides what runs on your device — and proves nothing leaves it — is open. Star the repo if you want to follow along, or clone it and check for yourself.

This reply was generated with the network off.

No request left the phone — the model is on the device, not a thin client for someone else's datacenter. 15 tokens/sec on an iPhone 12, measured. The whole point: nothing you type ever travels.
